summary: I discovered a lot about my family's past through the International Tracing Service. It was through their records, accessible at the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum, that I pieced together details like my birth date, birthplace, and camp number. These documents helped me understand how my family was tracked and kept together after the war.

The United States Holocaust Memorial Museum was crucial in my research about my family's history. They provide access to the International Tracing Service records, which helped me uncover the details of my birth and family separation after the war. Visiting the museum and using their archives gave me a deeper understanding of the refugee experience post-Holocaust.

I also found valuable records at the Center for Migration Studies, which focuses on migration history on this side of the Atlantic. Their archives complemented what I found in Europe and helped me understand the refugee experience from a broader perspective. It was important for me to see how migration was documented and managed after the war.

The UNRRA played a role in the camps where my family stayed after the war. I saw their name on some of the documents and index cards related to the camps in Germany. They were involved in the relief efforts for displaced persons like my family during that chaotic time.

IBM was involved in the Nazi system through their punch card technology, which was used to track labor and prisoners. I learned that companies like IBM had a role in the bureaucracy that managed people during the war. This connection showed me how industry was intertwined with the horrors of that time.

Ford Motor Company was mentioned as one of the German industries involved during the war. Though I didn't go into detail, I know that companies like Ford were part of the system that exploited labor during the Nazi era. It was part of the broader industrial complicity I learned about.

Philadelphia became my home after the war, where I ended up in Saint Basil's Orphanage. It was there that I grew up and where the local community helped displaced children like me. I also later learned that my sponsor lived on a street in Philadelphia, which connected me to my new life in the United States.

Saint Basil's Orphanage in Philadelphia was where I was cared for as a child after the war. The orphanage was supported by volunteers and donations, including a significant check from a Bishop who was moved by the children's plight. It was a place where many displaced children found shelter and hope.

The Vatican was involved through a letter sent by a Bishop in Philadelphia to a Vatican official, likely in Washington, D.C. This connection helped support Ukrainian families who did not want to be repatriated after the war. The Vatican's involvement showed the religious and humanitarian networks at work during that time.

Washington, D.C. was mentioned as the probable location of a Vatican official who received support letters from the Philadelphia Bishop. It was also the place where the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum is located, where I accessed important records. The city represents a hub for international and humanitarian efforts related to refugees.

The Third United States Army played a key role in relocating my family and others to safer camps. They helped move us to a model city in Cornberg, which had been a copper mine owned by Herman Göring. The Army also provided supplies and encouraged us to practice self-government, which was a unique experience for displaced persons.

My father celebrated Christmas according to the Julian calendar even while he was a prisoner of war. Despite the Nazis pointing rifles at him, he kept singing Christmas songs, believing that if he couldn't sing, life wasn't worth living. This calendar marked an important cultural and religious tradition for him during those harsh times.

My parents had connections with survivors from Buchenwald concentration camp, which was liberated by the Americans. Some Ukrainian political prisoners there were musicians and opera singers who helped organize resistance and spy networks. I also learned that after liberation, the Soviets used Buchenwald as a concentration camp again, which was a tragic continuation of suffering.

The Soviet Union was a place my family and many Ukrainians feared returning to after the war because of communist persecution. The Soviets would come to camps to forcibly repatriate people, but many resisted because they knew the harsh realities under communism. My family chose to move to the American zone to avoid Soviet control.

Hollendorf was the first camp my parents stayed in after the war. It was a diverse place with Poles, Russians, and Ukrainians, though the Ukrainians did not get along well with the Poles or Russians. This camp was part of the difficult early postwar period before moving to other camps like Cornberg.

Rodina, meaning 'homeland' in Russian, referred to the Soviet Union where many were forcibly repatriated. Ukrainians like my family did not want to return there due to communist oppression. The term symbolized the place people were pushed to go back to but resisted strongly.

The United States became my family's new home after the war. We were sponsored and resettled in places like Philadelphia, where I grew up in an orphanage. The U.S. also played a role in liberating camps and assisting displaced persons through various organizations and military efforts.

Herman Göring's name came up because the camp we moved to, Cornberg, was a model city for mine workers and had been a copper mine owned by him. The residents were given 48 hours to vacate so displaced persons like my family could move in. It was ironic that a place tied to a Nazi leader became a refuge for us.

Cornberg was a camp where my family lived after being relocated by the U.S. Third Army. It was a well-equipped place with running water, a wood stove, and a garden. We had some self-government, built a stage, warehouses, and churches, and tried to prepare for a democratic future while hoping to return home.

Leipheim was another camp where my family was moved after Cornberg because the residents said we had overstayed. It was more typical than Cornberg, and part of the ongoing displacement experience. The move was difficult but necessary as the camps were temporary solutions.

Eschwege was a nearby camp to Cornberg, but it was entirely Jewish. There, synagogues were established and Zionist agricultural work was done to prepare for emigration to Palestine. It was interesting to see how different displaced groups organized themselves with similar goals.

Palestine was the destination many Jewish refugees in camps like Eschwege prepared to go to through agricultural training. It represented hope for a new beginning after the war. The Zionist efforts in the camps were focused on making that emigration possible.

The Catholic Church was one of the religious communities active in the camps, including Cornberg. Despite minor differences, Catholics, Orthodox, and Baptists often worshiped together in the same church building. The Catholic majority usually went first in services, showing the religious dynamics in the camps.

The Eastern Orthodox Church was another faith community present in the camps where my family lived. They shared worship spaces with Catholics and Baptists, sometimes turning their icons around to accommodate different practices. This cooperation was part of the broader effort to maintain faith and culture in difficult times.

Baptists were also part of the religious groups in the camps, worshiping alongside Catholics and Orthodox Christians. Despite differences, they managed to share the same church buildings and participate in communal religious life. This ecumenical spirit helped maintain community bonds among displaced persons.

entity: United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration
summary: The United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration, or UNRRA, was involved in managing displaced persons camps and providing aid after the war. Their name appeared on documents related to the camps my family stayed in. They were part of the international effort to help refugees recover and resettle.

Camp Leipheim was where my family was relocated after Cornberg. It was a more typical displaced persons camp in Germany, marking another stage in our postwar journey. Life there was different, and it reflected the ongoing uncertainty for refugees at that time.

ORT was mentioned as one of the organizations involved in refugee rehabilitation and training after the war. They helped displaced persons gain skills and prepare for new lives. Their work was part of the broader support network for refugees like my family.

The Exodus ship was referenced in the context of Jewish refugees preparing to go to Palestine. It symbolizes the struggle and hope of displaced persons seeking a homeland after the war. Though I didn't have a direct connection, it was part of the larger refugee story I learned about.

Mike and Stefani was mentioned as part of the cultural or historical context related to displaced persons and refugees. It reflects stories of families and individuals navigating the postwar world. These narratives helped me understand the personal side of the refugee experience.

Bad Aibling was mentioned as one of the locations connected to displaced persons camps or refugee movements after the war. It was part of the network of camps in Germany where refugees lived before resettlement. Though not my family's primary camp, it was part of the larger context.

The American Friends Service Committee was one of the volunteer organizations helping refugees after the war. They worked on both sides of the Atlantic to support displaced persons like my family. Their efforts were vital in providing aid and hope during difficult times.

Baltimore was mentioned as another American city involved in refugee resettlement efforts. Like Philadelphia, it was part of the network where displaced persons found new homes in the United States. These cities played important roles in helping refugees start over.
